analysis.summary: The console error 'Cannot read properties of undefined (reading meta)' fires when adding a new FooterInfoSocialItem because Footer.tsx has a useEffect that subscribes to the FULL nested block tree via getBlocks(clientId) and reruns on every deeply-nested block change. When a new social item is inserted, this useEffect fires during the insertion process and can trigger replaceInnerBlocks in a race condition with the block creation lifecycle. The replaceInnerBlocks call propagates through WordPress core's block synchronization, which calls editEntityRecord on entity records that are not yet initialized.
analysis.rootCause: Footer.tsx useEffect at line 40-67 watches innerBlocks (full nested tree from getBlocks(clientId)). Any block change anywhere inside the Footer (including adding a deeply-nested FooterInfoSocialItem) triggers this effect. The effect calls replaceInnerBlocks during the insertion transaction, creating a race condition where editEntityRecord is dispatched before the new block's entity record is fully registered in the store.
errorChain: 1. User clicks Appender button in FooterInfoSocial to add a new FooterInfoSocialItem
2. WordPress block-editor begins the block insertion transaction
3. getBlocks(footerClientId) returns a new reference because the deep tree changed
4. Footer.tsx useEffect fires because innerBlocks dependency changed
5. The useEffect iterates TEMPLATE, finds existing blocks, compares order
6. During insertion, the transitional block state may cause isSameOrder check to fail OR needsUpdate to be true (if innerBlocks is momentarily empty or has a different structure)
7. replaceInnerBlocks fires, replacing the entire inner block tree mid-insertion
8. WordPress core's block synchronization dispatches editEntityRecord for the blocks being replaced
9. The entity record for the just-inserted social item is not yet in the core-data store
10. core-data.js reducer tries to access record.meta on undefined, throws TypeError
11. Second click works because the blocks are now fully registered in the store

secondaryIssues.description: The useEffect runs on EVERY nested block change (text edits, attribute changes, etc.) even though it only cares about direct children. This is a performance issue that also increases the surface area for race conditions.

changes.description: Replace the innerBlocks useSelect that watches the full nested tree with a selector that only returns direct children block names and clientIds. This prevents the useEffect from firing on deeply-nested changes.
changes.after: Use a custom selector that returns only the direct children's names and clientIds as a stable array. Use a shallowEqual comparison or serialize the children identifiers to prevent unnecessary reruns.

changes.description: Add an initialization guard to the useEffect so it only runs when the Footer block is first mounted or when direct children actually change (add/remove/reorder), not on every nested block modification.
changes.before: useEffect with innerBlocks dependency that fires on every nested change
changes.after: useEffect with stable dependency (direct children names/clientIds string) that only fires when structural changes occur to the Footer's immediate children

implementation.approach: Replace the broad getBlocks() subscription with a narrow selector that only tracks direct children structure

code.selector: Replace lines 35-67 with a two-part approach: (1) a useSelect that returns a serialized key of direct children names+clientIds, and (2) a useEffect that only fires when this key changes
pattern: // Only track direct children's names and clientIds to avoid rerunning on nested changes
// Use directChildrenKey as the dependency instead of directChildren (object reference)
// This ensures the effect only runs when children are added, removed, or reordered

steps: 1. Open the site editor and navigate to the Footer template part
2. Find the FooterInfoSocial section with existing social items
3. Click the Appender (+) button to add a new FooterInfoSocialItem
4. Verify NO console error appears on first click
5. Verify the new social item is added successfully
6. Select a social icon (e.g., Facebook) for the new item
7. Add a second social item and verify no errors
8. Verify existing social items still function correctly (link editing, icon changing)
9. Save the template part and reload -- verify all items persist
10. Open a regular page in the post editor that uses the footer template part -- verify no errors on page load
regressionChecks: Footer direct children (footer-info, footer-nav, footer-credits) are still enforced on initial load
Removing a direct child block still triggers re-creation
Reordering direct children still triggers correction
Editing text/attributes in nested blocks does NOT trigger the Footer useEffect

decisions.decision: Narrow the useSelect dependency from full nested tree to direct children structure key
decisions.evidence: The useEffect only operates on direct children (footer-info, footer-nav, footer-credits) but subscribes to the entire nested block tree. This causes unnecessary reruns and race conditions during deeply-nested block insertion.

decisions.decision: Use a serialized string key as useEffect dependency instead of the blocks array reference
decisions.evidence: getBlocks() returns a new array reference on every store update even when the direct children haven't changed. A serialized key (name:clientId pairs) provides stable dependency tracking.
